title: Methods & Overloading
description: Understand Java method mechanics — pass by value, varargs, return types, scope — and master method overloading resolution rules that generate tricky CSE110 exam questions.

sections.content: 🎯 Why Methods & Overloading Matter on Exams

CSE110 exams love testing two things: (1) **pass-by-value confusion** — students think Java passes objects by reference and are surprised when variables don't change, and (2) **overload resolution** — which version of a method gets called when multiple signatures match. Both concepts require you to understand the JVM's calling rules precisely, not approximately.
🏗️ Method Anatomy

A Java method has six parts: **access modifier**, **static/instance keyword**, **return type**, **method name**, **parameter list**, and **method body**. Every part affects how the method is called and what it can access. The **signature** — name + parameter types (not return type) — is what Java uses to distinguish overloaded methods.

sections.title: Method Structure Breakdown

sections.content: 📬 Pass By Value — Java's Only Calling Convention

Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. When you call a method, Java copies the argument's value into the parameter. For **primitives**, this means changes to the parameter do not affect the original. For **objects/arrays**, the value copied is the **reference** (memory address) — so the method can modify the object's contents, but cannot change what object the caller's variable points to.

rows: ❌ No — String is immutable; modifications create new String

sections.title: Pass By Value — All Three Cases

sections.label: 🚨 The Object Reference Misconception
sections.content: Many students say "Java passes objects by reference" — this is **incorrect** and leads to wrong exam answers. Java passes the **value of the reference** (the address). This means: YES, you can call `arr[0] = 5` and the caller sees the change. But NO, `arr = new int[]{5}` does **not** change what the caller's variable points to. The distinction is tested directly on exams.
🔁 Method Overloading

Method overloading lets you define **multiple methods with the same name** but different **parameter lists** (number, types, or order of parameters). The compiler selects which version to call at **compile time** based on the argument types — this is **static dispatch**. Overloading is different from **overriding** (which is runtime dispatch in subclasses).

rows: `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)`
`f(int a, double b)` vs `f(double a, int b)`
`add(int x, int y)` vs `add(int a, int b)`

sections.title: Overloading Resolution in Action

sections.label: Overload Resolution Order
sections.content: When no exact-type match exists, Java applies: (1) **widening** primitive conversion (byte→short→int→long→float→double), (2) **autoboxing** (int→Integer), (3) **varargs**. Java never narrows automatically. If a `long` is passed and there's only an `int` version, the `long` will **not** auto-narrow — it'll match `Object` or `double` via widening, or cause a compile error if no match exists.
🔢 Varargs — Variable Argument Lists

Varargs (`Type... name`) lets a method accept **zero or more** arguments of a given type. Under the hood, Java wraps them into an array. A method can have at most **one** varargs parameter, and it must be the **last** parameter. Varargs match only when no other signature fits more specifically.

sections.title: Varargs Mechanics

sections.content: 🌐 Variable Scope in Methods

**Scope** defines where a variable is accessible. In Java: **local variables** (declared inside a method/block) are only accessible within that block and must be explicitly initialized before use. **Parameters** are local to the method. **Static fields** are accessible anywhere in the class. Inner blocks can shadow outer variables, but Java prohibits re-declaring a local variable in a nested scope that already has that name.

sections.title: Scope Rules and Shadowing

sections.label: Local Variable Must Be Initialized
sections.content: Unlike class fields (which get default values: 0, false, null), **local variables** have **no default value** in Java. Using an uninitialized local variable is a **compile-time error**: `int x; System.out.println(x);` → *'variable x might not have been initialized'*. This is a common trap in exam code with conditional initialization paths.
🔁 Recursive Methods

A method is **recursive** when it calls itself. Every recursive method needs a **base case** (stopping condition) and a **recursive case** that moves toward it. Java allocates a new **stack frame** for each call — too deep recursion causes `StackOverflowError`. For CSE110, be able to trace recursive calls and predict output.

sections.title: Recursive Method Tracing

sections.label: Tracing Recursion on Exams
sections.content: Draw a **call stack** on paper. For `countdown(4)`: print 4, call countdown(3), print 3, ..., call countdown(0), print 0, call countdown(-1), return. For `countup`, the print happens **after** the recursive call returns, so output appears in reverse. The rule: print **before** recursion → normal order; print **after** recursion → reversed order.
🏋️ Practice Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Overload Resolution Trace
sections.answer:
int: 7
double: 7.0
two ints: 10
int: 4
double: 7.0
sections.explanation: `show(7)` → exact match with `show(int x)` → `int: 7`. `show(7.0)` → exact match with `show(double x)` → `double: 7.0`. `show(7, 3)` → exact match `show(int, int)` → `two ints: 10`. `show(b)` where b is byte → widening: byte widens to int (not double) — Java picks the narrowest fitting type → `int: 4`. `show(7.0f)` where 7.0f is float → no float overload → float widens to double (not int — widening never narrows) → `double: 7.0`.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Pass By Value Trace
sections.explanation: `a = 99` changes the local copy of x — x in main is still 1. `b[0] = 99` modifies element 0 of the original array through the shared reference — arr[0] becomes 99. `b = new int[]{0,0,0}` rebinds the local parameter b — arr in main still points to the original 3-element array. arr.length is still 3, arr[1] is still 2. `c = "changed"` rebinds local c — s in main still points to "original". String is also immutable so += would create a new String anyway. Output: 1, then 99 2 3, then original.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Overloading Ambiguity Error
sections.error: Line 10: `compute(3, 4)` — both arguments are int literals. Java could widen either argument: (3 as int, 4 widened to double) → matches first overload, OR (3 widened to double, 4 as int) → matches second overload. Neither is more specific, so the compiler reports: 'reference to compute is ambiguous' — compile-time error. Line 11 also causes an error: `compute(3.0, 4.0)` passes two doubles, and neither overload accepts (double, double), so no match exists — compile-time error.
sections.explanation: This is a **compile-time error** (ambiguity). When `compute(3, 4)` is called with two ints, the compiler finds two equally valid widening paths: widen second arg to double → first overload, or widen first arg to double → second overload. Neither is more specific so the call is ambiguous. Similarly `compute(3.0, 4.0)` has no (double, double) overload to match. Fix: add explicit (int,int) and (double,double) overloads that cover the missing cases.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Recursive Output Trace
sections.explanation: Trace the call stack: mystery(3) prints '3 ', calls mystery(2). mystery(2) prints '2 ', calls mystery(1). mystery(1) prints '1 ', calls mystery(0). mystery(0) prints '0 ' and returns. Back in mystery(1): prints '1 '. Back in mystery(2): prints '2 '. Back in mystery(3): prints '3 '. Full output: `3 2 1 0 1 2 3 `. The pattern: print going DOWN, then print again coming BACK UP the call stack — like a palindrome. There is a trailing space after the last 3.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Overloaded Calculator Methods
sections.approach: Define all five overloads. The tricky part is that `add(5)` could match `add(int a, int b)` if a second arg is provided — but with a single arg, only the varargs version applies (since no single-int signature exists). Be careful that `add(1, 2)` picks `add(int, int)` over varargs because exact match takes priority.
sections.explanation: Each overload is selected by exact argument type matching first, then widening, then varargs (lowest priority). `add(3,4)` → exact match `(int,int)` → returns 7. `add(1.5,2.5)` → exact `(double,double)` → 4.0. `add(1,2,3)` → exact `(int,int,int)` → 6. `add("Hi ","World")` → exact `(String,String)` → concatenation. `add(5)` → no single-int method exists → falls to varargs `(int...)` → 5. `add(1,2,3,4,5)` → 5 args, no 5-int overload, falls to varargs → 15. Note: `add(1,2,3)` goes to the explicit 3-arg version, not varargs — explicit overloads always beat varargs.

sections.content: 📌 Quick Reference Summary

items: **Method signature** = name + parameter types (in order); return type is NOT part of the signature
**Overloading** requires different parameter count or types; same name, same params but different return type → compile error
**Pass by value** (primitives): parameter is a copy; changes inside method do not affect caller
**Pass by reference value** (objects/arrays): parameter holds a copy of the reference; element/field mutations ARE visible to caller; reassigning the parameter is NOT
**String is immutable**: even though String is an object, `s = s + "x"` inside a method creates a new String and does not affect caller
**Overload resolution order**: (1) exact match → (2) widening → (3) autoboxing → (4) varargs; Java never auto-narrows
**Widening order**: byte → short → int → long → float → double; char → int
**Varargs**: must be last parameter; `int... nums` treated as `int[]` inside method; matched only when no other overload fits
**Local variable scope**: valid only within the block declared; nested block cannot re-declare a variable already in enclosing block
**Uninitialized locals**: compile error if used before assignment; class fields get defaults (0, false, null), locals do not
**Recursion**: every recursive method needs a base case; print before recursion → natural order; print after → reversed order
